# Transcrição: 01-WEBINAR

- **Fonte:** 01-WEBINAR
- **Duração:** 1:07:37
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Hey everybody and welcome to speaking through adjacency, making your content memorable, entertaining and impactful. A process that I teach people, a process that I have developed to help you deliver stories, content of any kind in a more memorable and impactful way. Speaking through adjacency is the idea that rather than creating sentences that lack storytelling elements or imagery, we're going to teach people, instruct people, inform people using storytelling as the primary means for delivering our messages. Speaking through adjacency is the idea that we're actually going to entertain our audiences while priming them for the lesson we're about to teach. We're actually going to get our audiences to be excited about what we're saying. We're going to get them to wonder about why we're saying it. We might make them laugh and cry along the way, but in the end we're going to be delivering a message. And many times that message is sober, serious and technical. Real stuff that applies to people in the workplace and even in teaching and even in your personal life. Real messages that we want to deliver, but rather than delivering them in ways that are quite honestly forgotten, white, round and flavorless, what I call word salad. Rather than delivering word salad, we're going to deliver messages through storytelling. We're going to infuse storytelling into everything that we say by speaking through adjacency in order to achieve this goal. So throughout this webinar, I'm going to teach you a few things and I'm going to teach primarily through example and then eventually I'm going to break it down into some specificity. But I think examples are really powerful. Opportunities to see how speaking through adjacency, changing the way that storytellers and speakers deliver messages and most importantly, the way in which audiences receive them. So let me give you an example. The first one. My wife, Alicia and I are at an educational conference back in September. We're both teachers. She teaches kindergarten and I teach fifth grade, but we're also very invested in storytelling and public speaking. I of course really care about storytelling, but so too does Alicia. She is the producer of our speak up storytelling shows. She's the host of our shows. She's been listening to my stories for more than a decade. She understands storytelling and public speaking well. So we're at this conference and there's a number of speakers delivering speeches and talks of all kinds. One of them is a student. She's about nine years old and she's hilarious. She actually dances in the midst of her talk. I don't really remember what she said, but I remember her making me feel really happy. But the two talks I want to center on are the first talk and the last talk of the day. In the first talk, an educational administrator comes onto the stage.

## [03:00 - 06:02]

He's got a lunchbox in his hand. It's one of those metal lunchboxes from the 1980s. My lunchbox from the 1980s was a Muppet Show lunchbox. You probably had one too if you're my age. It's battered. It looks like it's been used a thousand times. He puts it on a stool and he begins telling a story. He tells the story about how he's an immigrant, how he came to America with his parents at a very young age and they had no money. He talks about how his childhood was a struggle because of the poverty that he and his family experienced. He talked about how hard his parents worked, how both of them worked at least two jobs at a time in order to keep the family with a roof over their head and food in their refrigerator. The one thing that his family made sure, the one thing his parents guaranteed him every year was a new lunchbox because in his school, the lunchbox was sort of social equity. It was the means by which you could pass through the classroom door and feel good about yourself. Every year, it was a new topic on the lunchbox, a new feature on the lunchbox. For me, it was the Muppet Show. For him, it was whatever was popular that year. Somehow, despite the fact they had no money, his parents always made sure he had the right lunchbox so he had that social capital to move forward in his life. He never forgot it. It was the symbol for him about the struggle that his parents endured to guarantee that he would have the best education possible. He eventually becomes the first person in his family to go to college. He becomes a teacher and now today he's an administrator in a large educational system. But he still has that lunchbox. He looks at it all the time. It's on a shelf in his office. And it serves as a reminder about the sacrifices that his parents made for him in order to guarantee that he had the best chance for success in his life. And so when he finishes telling the story about the lunchbox and his parents and the poverty and the struggle and the success, he pauses for a minute. And then he looks at his audience, which is filled with about a thousand teachers, and he says, I want you to remember this story. I want you to remember that every single time a student walks into your classroom, they are walking in with the hopes and dreams of their parents on their back. But every parent, no matter what their socioeconomic status is making enormous sacrifices for their kids every day so that their kids can have more than they ever had, so that their opportunities are greater, their possibilities are endless. He says, I want you to think about that every single time a kid walks into your classroom, that they're not walking in alone. They're walking in with the sacrifices of their parents.

## [06:02 - 09:09]

He says, I want you to think about my lunchbox. I want you to think about it and remember it so that on the days when school seems hard, when teaching seems impossible and your students seem outrageously terrible, I want you to remember that these are small human beings for whom people are making enormous sacrifices and so too should you. The last speaker of the day was another administrator. He stood at the podium and he talked about the importance of data and education. He talked about how important it is to help kids bridge the achievement gap. I think he said that. I kind of don't remember anything he said except I remember he said data and I remember he said achievement gap. When both were finished speaking and the conference was over and Alicia was getting ready to stand up, I turned to her and I said what did you think of the speakers? She said the kid was amazing and I agree the kid was amazing. Brought genuine joy to our heart. Then she said, well the lunchbox guy I'm never going to forget. Then she said the lunchbox guy I'm never going to forget him. I agreed. That was three years ago and I can still see that lunchbox in my mind's eye as clear as day. Then I said what did you think about the last speaker? She said he did great. I said what did he say? This was two minutes after he had finished speaking. I said what did he say? My wife, a kindergarten teacher who's invested in storytelling and public speaking looked at me and said actually I don't really remember anything he said. She smiled and I said yeah, I don't remember anything he said either. The problem with that last speech was it was word salad. Salad is a word I use specifically because salad is good for you. I hate salad. I can't stand it. I wish I liked it. For every person in the world who looks down upon me because I don't like salad, do you think I'm really making this choice? I'm not. I would love to enjoy the salad bar but I don't. But I recognize how good salad is for you. I called his speech word salad because it sounded really good. The sentences were all complete. The grammar was excellent. His elocution was fantastic. It was a perfectly delivered speech of word salad, meaning it sounded great but ultimately it was forgotten almost immediately. As most salads are. Salad may be really good for you but what was the last salad you had that you can really remember? We remember great meals in our lives. We almost never remember salad even though it's really good. His speech was really good but failed to land in our memory for one simple reason.

## [09:09 - 12:10]

He didn't speak through adjacency, meaning he didn't tell a story. He didn't create any imagery in our minds. He didn't activate our imagination. He didn't cause us to see things that we would remember. He didn't take a story and tell it to us in a brilliant and beautiful and bold way in a way that connected with our hearts and our minds and then use that story as a pivot point, an example, a metaphor for the message he wanted to deliver. The first speaker did that perfectly. He told us an entertaining speech that made us laugh and honestly brought tears to my eyes. Whatever message he had to deliver, he connected the message to the story, to a lunchbox. That's why I'll never forget it. To this day, there are moments when kids in my class start to really annoy me as kids are wont to do. There are moments when I think to myself, you're making me crazy but I do know that there are human beings at home who are making enormous sacrifices so you can be here and so you can be prepared to learn. So I will make a sacrifice now and put up with your nonsense and try like hell to teach you. That is a lesson that has stuck with me because of a man and a lunchbox and a story. When we speak through adjacency, meaning we're going to speak about something without really speaking about it, at least initially, we open up hearts and minds and we create memorable content, the kinds of things that people will always remember. When we fail to speak with adjacency and instead we just speak in clear, coherent sentences, those sentences fall away and are forgotten. They're ultimately round, white, and flavorless, meaning they're just like everyone else's sentences. There's nothing special about them and there's nothing memorable about them. Speaking through adjacency allows us to become memorable, impactful, convincing, connecting storytellers who people will always be thinking about long after we're done speaking. That is what I mean by speaking with adjacency. Let me give you another example. About a decade ago, I'm speaking at Purdue University. I'm actually there teaching some classes on storytelling, helping professors learn how to tell stories in class, but there also happens to be at the time a conference on human trafficking. The conference organizer discovers that Matthew Dix, the storyteller and public speaker, is going to be on campus on the last day of the human trafficking conference. She calls me and she says, would you mind delivering the closing keynote speech for our human trafficking conference? I say yes, I'd love to. Dix go by and eventually I find myself on the campus of Purdue and I'm teaching my classes and the afternoon comes where I have to deliver the keynote.

## [12:10 - 15:11]

I'm standing outside the lobby and I'm waiting to speak and the organizer comes down and she introduces herself and thanks me for being there. She asks me, have you been doing any research on human trafficking? I said, oh no, none really, nothing at all. She has a look of sort of horror on her face. She can't believe it. She says, you've done no research at all? I said, no, nothing at all. It's fine. Everything's going to be great. This is not the way I should have handled it. I was being a little avaliar. I was exuding hubris in a place that I didn't need to but I was younger and foolish. So I was. I said, no, it's going to be fine. It's going to be great actually. She said, you don't really know anything about human trafficking. I said, well, I know what everybody knows. I know the basics. I know how terrible it is. I understand kind of how it functions. I know those things, but no, I didn't do any extra research. I'm no smarter than the average person when it comes to the topic. And she says, how are you going to deliver a keynote on a topic you know nothing about? And I said, just trust me. Now, she did not trust me, but I also knew that I was 10 minutes from speaking. There was no way she was going to get another keynote speaker. I was going to have to be the one. And she knew it too. She was pretty frustrated with me, but I knew it was going to be fine. And it was. So I entered the room. There was about a thousand people in this enormous conference room. I went up onto the stage. There was actually a podium and they wanted me to stand behind the podium. You should never stand behind the podium. The podium is a disaster for a bunch of reasons. First of all, if you have a podium, people think you're reading and I never want people to think I'm reading. I want people to know I'm speaking from the heart. Also, if you're standing behind a podium, it's harder to connect with an audience. You essentially become a head and maybe some shoulders like what I look like right now. And that's not great. I want to stand in front of my audience. I want to be a physical presence. So I took the microphone off the podium, much to their disgruntlement. They were really like, what are you doing? And I said, it's fine. It'll be fine. I kept saying all day, it's going to be fine. So I moved to the center of the stage, dragging this microphone on a cord behind me. And I began speaking. What I started to do was tell a story. I'm going to play that story for you now. It's a story that I told at the Moth a few years ago. I told basically this same story at the Human Trafficking Conference to begin my talk. It was a little longer at the Human Trafficking Conference. There's things in the story that are dropped out because the Moth has a six minute time limit. So it was a little more robust. It was probably the 10 minute version of the story, but it's essentially the story that I'm going to play for you now. I told this story and then I did something else. But let's start by watching the story. I like at least that much noise. Twice as much noise for Matthew Dix as he comes up to the stage. Matthew Dix. Thank you.

## [15:11 - 18:11]

It was bad when I threw my shoe at my third grade student and hit her in the head. And it was the first day of school, which is the only day of the entire school year when kids want to go home and tell their parents what happened in school that day. And the principal's daughter was in my classroom that year. So there was no way he wasn't going to find out what happened. So I sat at my desk and I prepared a vigorous defense. I was going to explain to him that I did not mean to hit her in the head with my shoe. I meant to throw it high for effect. And it was only because she couldn't stay in her seat, which was the reason I threw the shoe in the first place, that she was hit. So really it was a natural consequence. It was not my fault. I was also going to point out that I was wearing a loafer that day, which while technically a shoe on the periodic table of footwear is much more closely aligned to a slipper. So I didn't really do any damage in hitting her. And so with my defense ready, I waited. And I waited. And I waited. And he never came to talk to me. So after a week, I went to the kid who I hit and I said, you didn't tell your parents that I hit you with a shoe? And she said, no. And I said, why? She said, because they would have found out I was misbehaving and I don't need that kind of trouble. And then I went to the principal's daughter and I said, you didn't tell your father that I hit Roxy with a shoe? And she said, I don't tell my father anything. And at that moment, I knew I was going to have a great school year because these kids came into my room with my personal belief system fully intact within them. And by November, like I had made meaningful connections with every kid in that class except one. And her name was Lisa. Lisa was a short girl with the most coke bottle-ish coke bottle glasses you've ever seen. She had long black hair that she pulled down over her eyes like a curtain. Lisa was shy. Lisa was almost invisible if you didn't look closely. And by November, I had run out of tricks to try to get Lisa out of her shell and to talk. And I'd resign myself to the fact that this is a quiet kid who's going to pass through my classroom quietly. And then one Friday, I was collecting homework, going from desk to desk to pick it up. And the kids were giving me a hard time. They were telling me how stupid the assignment had been and what a waste of time it was. And I got frustrated and I finally shouted out, bring out your dad, bring out your dad. I'm standing next to Lisa's desk and she looks up and says, Monty Python and the Holy Grail? And I say, yes! You know Monty Python and the Holy Grail?

## [18:11 - 21:13]

And she parts her little curtain a little bit. And she says, bring me a shrubbery. This little eight-year-old girl knew Monty Python and the Holy Grail. It made sense that I knew it from like 18 to 22. I'd watched the movie like a thousand times. But this little girl knew the movie and even better, her comedic timing was perfect. We started bantering back and forth. She'd throw out a line. I'd throw out a line. She'd say like, I fart in your general direction. And she started talking for the first time. And then the kids started asking like, what are you guys doing? And I said, it's Monty Python and the Holy Grail. It's the greatest movie of all time, but it's totally inappropriate so you should not see it. And so she continued. There was a moment when a kid came in from recess with a cut on his finger. And I didn't like kids go to the nurse ever unless they're already dead. So he was like going back and forth with me. And she walked by. She paused. She looked at it and said, it is but a flesh wound. And just walked on. And I'm like, she's a genius. And then this amazing thing happens. Four boys go home for the weekend, have a sleepover, and somehow they get their parents to rent the Monty Python and the Holy Grail. And they come in on Monday and they can quote a little bit of the movie, but more importantly, they understand that Lisa is funny. And she starts talking more. And then one by one by one, every kid in my class finds a way to see Monty Python and the Holy Grail. And every time they come back into the classroom, we cheer them. And Lisa gains stature every time. And she starts writing. She starts writing jokes which are really terrible and not funny. But third graders are so stupid and they think they're funny. And she's perceived as a funny person. And then the principal comes into my room and now he's mad. He says to me, my daughter tells me she's the only one in your class who hasn't seen Monty Python and the Holy Grail. What is going on? And I said, I had nothing to do with it. It's an inappropriate movie and I do not encourage it. Sounds like you have a problem. And eventually even she sees the movie. And then March comes and Lisa's parents come in for a parent teacher conference. They sit across my desk and they tell me that Lisa's entire life has changed. That she goes home and she loves school and she can't wait to get back for the first time in her life. They tell me that I'm the best teacher. The guy who hit a girl in the head with a shoe is the best teacher ever. And I believe it for two days. And then I'm walking down the hall and I passed Stephanie, a fifth grader who I had in third grade. Stephanie was like Lisa. She did not talk. Her friend Keanu used to talk for her. She was so quiet. I passed Stephanie and I realized right away I never found her Monty Python and the Holy Grail because I gave up on her. And then I remember Joseph from eight years ago, my first class. I can't remember the sound of Joseph's voice because I don't know if he ever even spoke

## [21:13 - 24:19]

that year. And I think about how I didn't find his Monty Python and the Holy Grail either. I think about Lisa and I don't think of a success anymore. I did not save Lisa if anything Monty Python did, but really it was dumb luck. I got aggravated in class and shouted out a line from a movie and I connected with a kid. But for eight years all of those quiet kids in my class I gave up on. So when I think about Lisa today, my mind jumps right to Stephanie and Joseph and all those other quiet kids who I let down who I gave up on. So she serves for me not as success, but as a reminder that I can never ever let that happen again. Thanks. So I tell that story, which doesn't sound like it has anything to do with human trafficking, which is the key to speaking with adjacency. When we speak with adjacency, we first tell a story that does not feel related to the topic at hand, that is not specifically addressing the message that we want to deliver. The purpose in telling a story when we're speaking with adjacency is not to land the message, not to teach the content. It is not to do any of those things. It is a story that is somehow related to the topic at hand without it being obvious. So I tell that story. When the story finishes, I pause for a moment. I allow that story to sit with them. I know what they're thinking. They're thinking that was funny. That was interesting. I don't know if it was touching, but how the hell does that relate to what we do? Which is where I want them to be. I want them to be entertained, but also wondering, where is he going with this? Because when an audience is wondering, where is he going with this? That means they want to hear the next sentence. I want my audiences wondering at all times. I want them to be entertained because their hearts and their minds are now open and ready to receive a message. They don't even realize it, but they are. And now they're ready to receive a message directly related to the story they just heard. And so when I finish that story, I tell the people at the Human Trafficking Conference, that story and the problem I had when it came to Lisa is the same problem that you experience every day. I said, when people like us, teachers, people saving other human beings from human trafficking, when we are in the human being business, meaning our actions on our decisions impact the lives of actual human beings, we can never afford to do what I did to Stephanie and Joseph and all of those kids who I ignored for those years, those kids I gave up on, those kids who I assumed would not be coming out of their shells during my school year.

## [24:19 - 27:19]

I assumed that I didn't have any more tricks or tools in my tool belt to get Stephanie and Joseph and all those kids out of their shell. And instead, I should have been relentlessly trying at all times. That is the struggle that we have as people in the human being business. We are not making widgets. We are not designing platforms. We are not trying to make profits for a company. We are doing real work. We're doing the work that changes human beings' lives, which means when we feel like we need to quit because we are not making any progress, that's when we need to bear down and try even harder. That means that when some fool comes along and tells us it takes a long time to steer a large ship in a new direction, we have to say no, because our ship is not filled with widgets and profits and dividends. Our ship is filled with actual human beings. And when the ship is filled with human beings, you steer that ship as quickly as you damn well can. You don't let anyone tell you it's going to take a while to make change happen, not when human beings are at stake. That is the message I delivered. I delivered more of that message than what I just gave you now, but that is essentially what I did. Basically what I did was I told them the story about Lisa. I opened up their hearts and minds. I entertained them and I caused them to wonder, where is he going with this? And then I showed them where I was going. I told a story about how I gave up on a kid and I learned that when you're in the human being business, you can never give up on a kid. And then I related it to their work. I honored the job they were doing. I entrusted them with the job they were doing and I encouraged them to stand up to all the all stupidity and injustice and bureaucracy whenever necessary because we're in the human being business. When I finished, everyone stood up and I got a round of applause like I've never seen before. When I made it back to that organizer, she was crying. She was crying for two reasons. One she said she felt terrible that she doubted me and two she was crying because of what I said. But I'd gone into that talk and spoken more about human trafficking after three days of human trafficking. No one would remember what I said. I would have been just another talk about human trafficking in a three-day conference about human trafficking. So rather than being round, white and flavorless, rather than saying what was expected, I spoke with adjacency. I asked myself what's the story that I have in my repertoire of stories that can apply to the work that these wonderful people are doing. And that's what I did. I spoke through adjacency.

## [27:19 - 30:21]

And because of it, I still hear from people at that conference to this day about how they still think about Lisa. And when they hear Monty Python and the Holy Grail, they still think about my talk, which is another brilliant and wonderful thing about speaking through adjacency. When we speak through adjacency, when we tell stories about the content we're trying to deliver, the messages we're trying to deliver, when we speak through storytelling, we create imagery in people's minds and we grab artifacts from the world and we make use of them in our own content, which essentially creates memory stones out of those artifacts. When people at that conference watch Monty Python and the Holy Grail, they think about me. They think about Lisa. And they are inspired again because I've created something that reminds them of the moment we shared 10 years ago at Purdue University. It's the same way every single time I see a metal lunchbox, the kind of lunchbox I had from the 1980s, my mucket lunchbox, every single time I see a metal lunchbox. I think about that man. I think about his immigrant family. And I am reminded that every single student walks into my classroom with the hopes of that family and the sacrifices of that family on that child's back. When we speak through adjacency, we are not speaking with word salad. We are not assembling beautiful sentences with accurate punctuation delivered with excellent elocution that can instantly be forgotten. Instead, we are telling stories that are memorable because we create images in the minds, we open hearts in minds, and we create artifacts in the world, touchstones that people can look at, remember, and think about the story they once heard. That is the beauty of speaking through adjacency. So how do you do it? I've given you two examples. Now let's talk about how you do it. How do we find the stories we need? How do we deploy them throughout our speeches, our talks, our all hands, our keynotes, all of the content that we deliver to people every day? How do we deploy them in such a way that we can be as memorable ourselves as that lunchbox administrator was on that day? Let's talk about how. Okay, so how do we speak through adjacency? Let's talk about the specific ways that you can accomplish this goal. This image is a great way to remember it. Essentially, this image tells us we're going to speak about one thing, and at the same time we're going to speak about another thing, but the audience doesn't know we're speaking about the other thing, that they're really the same thing. But they're not aware of this until we bring the two ideas together until we fuse them as one. So if we take a look at the lunchbox story, that is a lunchbox story, but what it really

## [30:21 - 33:24]

is, it's a story about parental sacrifice. The audience doesn't know it at first though. They're being entertained. Their hearts and minds are being opened. They're connecting with the speaker. They're enjoying the story. They're wondering where this is going. All of those things are great. And then we fuse it with the idea of parental sacrifice, that every time a kid walks into your classroom, they're carrying all of the sacrifices of those parents with them. So that is how we're going to speak through adjacency. Or for example, we can take a look at Monty Python and Annalise. Her name, by the way, was really Annalise. Changed her name years ago when I was telling the story. Now she's grown up. So Monty Python and Annalise is the same as human trafficking. I'm telling a story about my treatment of a student, but what I'm really telling, what I know that the audience doesn't know yet, is I'm really talking about human trafficking, the importance of being in the human business and how you must never give up on humans when you're in that business. The audience doesn't know what I'm talking about. They're being entertained. Their hearts and minds are being open. They're connecting with me. They're starting to believe in me. They like me. And then I fuse the two ideas together and boom, that is speaking through adjacency. So how do we accomplish this goal? Well, recently I was working with another client and it was a pretty good example of how this can happen. So when we speak through adjacency, what we're asking ourselves is essentially one of three questions. We're asking ourselves what the theme, meaning or message of our content is. So we're trying to determine what am I trying to say and what is that thing that I'm trying to say is theme, meaning or message. Once we understand what that is, we can connect theme, meaning and message to a story we already have. Ideally, a story about ourselves, but sometimes a story about someone else in the world or some other thing in the world. Ideally about ourselves because people want stories about the person who's speaking. They want that vulnerability, that connectivity. Sometimes that's not possible, but oftentimes it is. So we're thinking about theme, meaning and message. Let me explain to you what that looks like. So I'm working with this guy, a contract manager at a large technical company, a large technical company that you touch every day, one of these big tech giants in the world. And David has some goals as the contract manager. So he's got three goals in particular. His first goal is, and I'm going to call this company the ACME company to sort of protect David's identity and the company's identity. But essentially what this company doing is it says an ACME device should be the same quality as every other ACME device, no matter where it is designed or manufactured. This is a company that is now beginning to send the design and manufacture of their products to other companies to get those companies to design and manufacture for them so that they can focus on other things. So one of the goals is to ensure that whether ACME is still making the product or some other company is making the product, that always the quality is going to be the same. He wants that message to be delivered in a 90 minute keynote he's giving to his team. So that's the first goal he has. The second goal is we must include every detail and every contract or our partners won't meet

## [33:24 - 36:26]

our expectations. When we send these designs and this product creation to some other company for us, we have to make sure that the contracts we write are exceedingly specific because our partners just aren't going to do what we don't tell them to do. So we have to be so specific in our directions and he wants them to understand that specificity is more important than almost anything in the world. They have to make sure that every single thing they want is in the contract. And the third goal is we must be more willing to delegate design to our partners and delegate effectively so we can focus on more profitable projects. He's trying to convince people that this is all a good idea, that it's okay that we're sending our design and our manufacturing of products to other companies. We have other things that we can be focused on. Other companies can get this work done for us. Those are his three goals he brings to me and he says, can we put storytelling into these three goals? And I say absolutely through adjacency. And so what do we do? Okay, so Dave, I'm my contract manager. Remember his goal number one was an ACME device should be the same quality as every other ACME device no matter where it's designed or manufactured. So Dave and I have a discussion about what we think the theme meaning or message and we decide on theme for this one. And we decide the theme we're looking for in this goal is consistency, equity and reputation. Essentially, there needs to absolutely be consistency between all the products despite the fact that they may be manufactured or designed in another place. They have to be consistent. There needs to be equity across the businesses. The reputation of ACME needs to be protected even if the product is being built somewhere else. And so the story that I proposed to Dave, the one we're going to speak through adjacency when I give my example is of my daughter Clara on her first day of kindergarten. And this is a photo of Clara on her first day of kindergarten saying goodbye to her brother Charlie for the very first time. She's off to her first day of kindergarten. And what I tell Dave is when I bring Clara to kindergarten on the first day, I don't know who her teacher is going to be. We arrive at the playground and there's three kindergarten teachers for whom Clara may be on the roster. The first teacher is an older lady. She is not smiling. And I know this kind of teacher being a teacher for a long time. I sort of eyeball this woman and realize, okay, she is the veteran. She is the one who is not the friendliest, not the happiest, not the funniest, but she's going to get the job done. She knows the best way to do everything. She knows the way to streamline the curriculum. She knows how to handle kids. This lady gets the job done. The second teacher is brand new, first year, which can make a lot of parents nervous. Doesn't make me nervous though, because I know the truth about brand new teachers. They work 10 times harder than any other teacher in the building. when I actually speak with adjacency, meaning I create this metaphor with an actual story from my life, which is what we should be doing. ## [54:34 - 57:40]

Steve Jobs believes in speaking through adjacency, too. I'd like you to listen to this. This is Steve Jobs speaking through adjacency, but kind of failing a little bit. We'll talk about why the great Steve Jobs fails when it comes to speaking through adjacency after you watch this clip. One of the things that really hurt Apple was after I left, John Scully got a very serious disease. And that disease, I've seen other people get it, too. It's the disease of thinking that a really great idea is 90% of the work. And that if you just tell all these other people, here's this great idea, then of course they can go off and make it happen. And the problem with that is that there's just a tremendous amount of craftsmanship in between a great idea and a great product. And as you evolve that great idea, it changes and grows. It never comes out like it starts because you learn a lot more as you get into the subtleties of it. And you also find there's tremendous trade-offs that you have to make. I mean, there are just certain things you can't make electrons do. There are certain things you can't make plastic do or glass do. And as you get into, or factories do, or robots do, and as you get into all of these things, designing a product is keeping 5,000 things in your brain, these concepts, and fitting them all together in kind of continuing to push, to fit them together in new and different ways to get what you want. And every day you discover something new that is a new problem or a new opportunity to fit these things together a little differently. And it's that process that is the magic. And so we had a lot of great ideas when we started. But what I've always felt that a team of people doing something they really believe in is like when I was a young kid, there was a widowed man that lived up the street. And he was in his 80s. He's a little scary looking. And I got to know him a little bit. I think he might have paid me to cut his mow his lawn or something. And one day he said, come on into my garage, I want to show you something. And he pulled out this dusty old rock tumbler. It was a motor and a coffee can and a little band between them. And he said, come on with me. We went out to the back and we got just some rocks, some regular old ugly rocks. And we put them in the can with a little bit of liquid and a little bit of grit powder. And we closed the can up and he turned this motor on. He said, come back tomorrow. And his can was making a racket as the stones were. And I came back the next day and we took, we opened the can and we took out these

## [57:40 - 1:00:39]

amazingly beautiful polished rocks. Um, the same common stones that had gone in through rubbing against each other like this, creating a little bit of friction, creating a little bit of noise, had come out these beautiful polished rocks. And that's always been in my mind, my metaphor for a team working really hard on something they're passionate about is, is that it's through the team, through that group of incredibly talented people bumping up against each other, having arguments, having fights sometimes, making some noise and working together. They polish each other and they polish the ideas and what comes out are these really beautiful stones. I think what we just saw was Steve Jobs speaking through adjacency, but he does something wrong. Did you pick up on what he did wrong? Did you see the thing that he did that made it less effective than what I've been teaching? If he didn't, here's what it is. Jobs, unfortunately delivers the message first and then the story serves as an example for the message, which is an effective way to teach admittedly. It works. It works for sure. People remember this talk. I know they do. I've heard it many times people say, Oh, it's like what jobs does when it comes to the rocks and they say not quite. Here's the difference. What jobs does is teaches you a lesson and then gives you an example to support the lesson. That's not speaking through adjacency. The problem is jobs steals away all of the stakes, suspense and surprise. The storytelling. The reason speaking with adjacency is so powerful is because what we do is we start with the story and we don't explain why we're telling the story. We don't give the lesson. We don't reveal the theme, meaning or message initially. We know what the theme, meaning and messages as the presenter, the speaker, the storytelling. But the audience doesn't know. And that's crucial because here's what I believe. Here's what I believe with all of my heart. I believe that it's that moment when the two things come together and connect in a surprising and stunning way. That is when messages become most memorable and most impactful. What jobs needed to do was to tell the story of the rock tumbler first. Tell the story about the man taking the rocks and putting them in the top of the building, taking the rocks and putting them in the tumbler and coming back and finding the smooth stones. And he tells that story. And after he's finished telling the story, he pauses and he says, people are like rocks. They're just like rocks. They need to tumble around. They need to bang off each other a little bit. They need to experience conflict and strife. They need to disagree. They need to get into the mix and eventually a team can be well rounded. In the same way those stones become smooth and round.

## [1:00:40 - 1:03:43]

A team can have the same thing happen, but it can only be happening if they're battling, they're banging off each other. They're having disagreements. They're finding common ground. They're struggling to find common ground. The magic of speaking with adjacency is the magic of surprise. It is the, why is this person talking about this? And then, wow, that's why this person is talking about this. Now I understand completely. There's research that shows that surprise increases brain plasticity, meaning when you're actually surprised when something is surprising to you, it's a moment of maximum education or maximum open-mindedness, or it's the moment when you are the most prepared and in tune for learning. So we want to surprise our audiences. It's in the moments of surprise that we get to jam our hearts and minds into their hearts and minds. It's the moment when they're the most open to learn. It's the moment when they're most open to receiving our message. Jobs fails because he steals the surprise. We understand what the rock tumbler is while he's telling it. We already know the purpose of it. Aaron Sorkin said, the worst thing you can ever do to an audience is tell them something they already know. As Steve Jobs is talking about the rock tumbler, you know why he's talking about it. He's just giving an example to something he already said. It's interesting. Compelling. It's fine. Can you imagine what would have happened if you had flipped it? Steve Jobs is talking about rocks and a rock tumbler and smoothing rocks. Why? Why is he talking about this? Oh, he's talking about how teams function. He's talking about the importance of people grinding against each other a little bit. He's talking about the importance of people engaging in discourse and discord and disagreement in order to become a well-rounded, smooth, fast running, highly effective team. It's the, oh, I understand why he's talking about this. That leads to the most memorable experiences for people. So Jobs fails. I don't think Jobs failed on purpose. I don't think it was poor planning. I think what happened was he was explaining a process about teamwork to an interviewer, and he got the sense the interviewer wasn't really getting it. And so he sought out for an example that might land the idea clearly. And that works. That's great. That is, I'm explaining something. They don't understand it. I'm going to offer a metaphor. I'm going to pivot. I'm going to give a story that explains what I was just talking about. Fine. It works really well, but it's not speaking with adjacency because it lacks the surprise. We already know why he's talking about the rock tumbler, and that makes it less exciting, less entertaining, less engaging, less connective, less surprising, all of those lessons. Speaking through adjacency at its heart is the idea that we're going to tell a story

## [1:03:43 - 1:06:44]

and we're not going to tell the audience why we're telling the story. We're going to tell a story in hopes of entertaining our audience, engaging them and connecting to them. And when that story is done, they will feel good. Their hearts will be open. Their minds will be open, and they will be wondering why did you just tell us that? And that is the moment when you pivot and you bring two ideas into one and the brain explodes and they go, I see a thing that I did not see before. I see a thing that I could not see before, but now it's so clear to me. That is the magic of speaking through adjacency. We do it simply by asking ourselves what's the theme, meaning, or message of the content we are delivering, the message we are trying to land, what is the theme, meaning, and message, and then what story do we have? Hopefully about ourselves that matches either the theme, meaning, or message. We accomplish those two things and now we're speaking through adjacency and now we're memorable. So that's the goal. The next time you have an all hands, the next time you have a keynote, the next time you're meeting with a team, the next time you're selling to a client, the next time you're standing in front of a group of students, and you're delivering content or a sales pitch or a pivot in the company's direction or a new set of values, a new strategy, whatever you're planning on delivering to the audience before you, ask yourself, what is the theme, meaning, or message attached to this content? And then ask yourself, what story can I tell? What story can I tell that is aligned with that theme, meaning, and message, a story that they will enjoy, be entertained by, have their hearts and minds open, maybe laugh while they're telling the story, right? Do all of those great things that storytellers do. And then when you get to the end of the story and the audience is thinking, that was great, but why then you pivot and you say, this is why and you explain exactly why you chose that story to deliver this message. And then you're memorable. You're unforgettable. People will remember your lunchbox. People will remember your apples. People will remember pickles on a plate. People will remember a magician, kindergarten teacher. They will remember all of those things because you will have attached your message to a story, to physical artifacts, to imagery in the brain, to your audience's own imagination. That is an exceptionally powerful way to speak through adjacency.

## [1:06:44 - 1:07:37]

And in order to do any of it as always, you must have stories to tell. So find stories through homework for life, through first, last, best, worst, through three, two, one, through all of the activities that I have, through all of the strategies that I teach, find stories, the best speakers in the world, the ones who speak with adjacency most often, the ones who do it the best are the ones who have the most stories to tell. So gather your wool, find your stories, make a giant pile of them so that the next time you have to speak about something, you can pluck one of those stories off the pile that matches the meaning and message, and you too can begin speaking through adjacency. Good luck.
